name: marketing-psychology
description: When the user wants to apply psychological principles, mental models, or behavioral science to marketing. Also use when the user mentions 'psychology,' 'mental models,' 'cognitive bias,' 'persuasion,' 'behavioral science,' 'why people buy,' 'decision-making,' or 'consumer behavior.' This skill provides 70+ mental models organized for marketing application.
capabilities: Apply 70+ mental models from psychology and behavioral science to marketing challenges
Identify which cognitive biases and psychological principles influence customer decisions
Design pricing strategies using anchoring, framing, and mental accounting principles
Create persuasion sequences using reciprocity, commitment, and social proof ethically
Diagnose conversion problems using the AIDA funnel and BJ Fogg behavior model
Build growth strategies based on compounding, network effects, and flywheel principles
author: coreyhaines31
securitySummary: All 26 static findings are false positives. The 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' detections triggered on marketing psychology terms like 'Mimetic Desire', 'Barbell Strategy', 'Availability Heuristic', and 'Network Effects' which are legitimate behavioral science concepts. The 'System reconnaissance' findings triggered on cognitive psychology terms like 'Inversion', 'Loss Aversion', and 'Door-in-the-Face' which are standard mental models. This is a text-based skill with no code execution, network access, or file system operations.
valueStatement: Marketing often fails because it ignores why people actually make decisions. This skill provides 70+ proven mental models from psychology and behavioral science to help you understand customer behavior, craft persuasive messaging, and influence decisions ethically.
limitations: Does not provide specific copy or creative assets; only psychological guidance and frameworks
Does not guarantee marketing outcomes; effectiveness depends on execution and context
Does not include A/B testing or data analysis; focuses on theoretical frameworks only
Does not replace market research or customer interviews; supplements rather than substitutes

useCases.targetUser: Conversion rate optimization specialists and growth marketers
useCases.title: Diagnose Low Conversion Rates
useCases.description: Apply Hick's Law, Activation Energy, and BJ Fogg Behavior Model to identify friction points in your funnel and understand why customers are not completing desired actions.

useCases.title: Design Persuasive Pricing Pages
useCases.description: Use anchoring, decoy effect, and mental accounting principles to structure pricing tiers that guide customers toward your target plan.

useCases.title: Write Ethical Persuasive Copy
useCases.description: Apply reciprocity, scarcity, social proof, and commitment principles to create copy that influences decisions while respecting customer autonomy.

promptTemplates.title: Apply Psychology to a Specific Challenge
promptTemplates.prompt: I'm facing this marketing challenge: [describe your situation]. Which mental models from behavioral science would help me approach this? Explain the psychology behind 2-3 relevant models and give specific application examples.
promptTemplates.scenario: When you need psychological insights for a marketing problem

promptTemplates.title: Analyze Customer Decision Journey
promptTemplates.prompt: Map the customer journey for [product/situation]. Use the AIDA funnel, commitment & consistency, and peak-end rule to identify opportunities to improve the experience and increase conversions. What specific psychological interventions would help at each stage?
promptTemplates.scenario: When you want to understand and optimize customer decision points

promptTemplates.title: Create Persuasion Strategy
promptTemplates.prompt: Create an ethical persuasion strategy using these principles: [list principles like scarcity, reciprocity, authority, social proof]. For each, give a specific tactic I could implement this week.
promptTemplates.scenario: When you need actionable persuasion tactics for a campaign

promptTemplates.title: Apply Behavioral Science to Pricing
promptTemplates.prompt: My current pricing is [describe]. Apply anchoring, charm pricing, decoy effect, and mental accounting to suggest improvements. Show how to frame each price point for maximum appeal.
promptTemplates.scenario: When you need to optimize pricing strategy using psychology

bestPractices: Start with the specific behavior you want to influence rather than the model. Let the psychology follow the business goal.
Test one principle at a time when possible. A/B test scarcity vs. social proof rather than both together.
Apply ethical constraints first. The goal is persuasion, not manipulation. Always consider long-term trust over short-term conversion.
antiPatterns: Using artificial scarcity or fake urgency that customers will recognize and resent once they learn the truth.
Overwhelming users with too many choices; the Paradox of Choice shows fewer options often convert better.
Ignoring the full customer journey. Applying psychological principles only at checkout while ignoring earlier friction loses conversions.

faq.question: What is the difference between marketing psychology and behavioral economics?
faq.answer: Marketing psychology focuses on how psychological principles apply to marketing and sales. Behavioral economics studies how psychological factors affect economic decisions. For marketing purposes, they overlap significantly. This skill draws from both fields to provide practical frameworks.

faq.question: Are these psychological principles ethical to use?
faq.answer: Yes, these principles describe how people naturally think and decide. Using them ethically means helping customers make decisions they would make anyway, just more clearly. The skill emphasizes authentic scarcity, genuine value demonstration, and respect for customer autonomy.

faq.question: How do I know which mental model to apply?
faq.answer: Start with your specific challenge: low conversions, price objections, urgency, or retention. The Quick Reference table in SKILL.md maps challenges to relevant models. You can also ask the skill directly which models apply to your situation.

faq.question: Do these principles work across different cultures?
faq.answer: Some principles are universal (loss aversion, reciprocity) while others vary by culture. The skill provides foundational models; adapt them based on your specific audience and test with real customers from that culture.

faq.question: How many psychological principles should I apply at once?
faq.answer: Focus on 2-3 principles that directly address your specific challenge. Applying too many creates confusion. The Paradox of Choice applies to messaging too. Test systematically rather than overwhelming users.

faq.question: Can these models predict campaign outcomes?
faq.answer: These models explain why certain approaches work but do not predict specific outcomes. They increase probability of success based on behavioral science research. Always test with real customers through A/B testing or experiments.
